title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to target. You may not use the same element twice.
examples: Input: nums = [2, 7, 11, 15], target = 9Output: [0, 1]
approach: nums[0] + nums[1] = 2 + 7 = 9. Use a hash map to achieve O(n) time.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
description: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring that contains no repeating characters.
examples: Input: s = "abcabcbb"Output: 3
approach: The longest substring without repeats is "abc". Use a sliding window with a character index map to keep the window valid in O(n).

title: Valid Parentheses
description: Given a string s, determine if it is valid. A string is valid if every open bracket is closed by the same type of bracket in the correct order, and no open bracket is left unclosed.
examples: Input: s = "{[()]}"Output: trueInput: s = "(]"Output: false
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Product of Array Except Self
description: Given an integer array nums, return an array answer where answer[i] equals the product of all elements of nums except nums[i].
approach: Build a left-product array and a right-product array, then multiply them together.

title: Trapping Rain Water
description: Given an array height representing an elevation map where each bar has width 1, compute how much water can be trapped between the bars after rain.
approach: Use the two-pointer approach: maintain left_max and right_max. At each step, water at position i = min(left_max, right_max) - height[i].

title: Minimum Window Substring
description: Given strings s and t, return the minimum window substring of s that contains every character of t (including duplicates). If no such window exists, return an empty string.
examples: Input: s = "ADOBECODEBANC", t = "ABC"Output: "BANC"
approach: Expand the right pointer until the window is valid, then shrink from the left to find the minimum valid window.

title: Merge Intervals
description: Given an array of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals and return an array of non-overlapping intervals covering the entire range.
approach: Sort by start time. Iterate and merge when the current interval's start is ≤ previous end.

title: Rotate Array
description: Given an integer array nums, rotate the array to the right by k steps. Try to solve it in O(1) extra space.
examples: Input: nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7], k = 3Output: [5, 6, 7, 1, 2, 3, 4]Hint: Reverse the full array, then reverse the first k elements, then reverse the remaining n-k elements.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
topics: s contains only lowercase English letters.
description: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s.
examples: Input: s = "babad"Output: "bab" (or "aba")Input: s = "racecar"Output: "racecar"
approach: For each character (and each pair), expand outward while characters match. O(n²) time, O(1) space.

title: Linked Lists
description: Prepare and solve the coding question: Linked Lists asked in JPMORGAN recruitment.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Reverse Linked List
description: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list and return the new head.
examples: Input: [1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5]Output: [5 → 4 → 3 → 2 → 1]Hint: Use three pointers: prev, curr, next. Iterative solution runs in O(n) time and O(1) space.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Detect Cycle in Linked List
topics: Floyd's Cycle DetectionConstraints: 0 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 10⁴.
description: Given the head of a linked list, return true if there is a cycle, otherwise false. Solve in O(1) extra space.
examples: Input: [3 → 2 → 0 → -4], pos = 1 (tail connects to index 1)Output: true
approach: Use slow and fast pointers. If they meet, a cycle exists.

title: Merge Two Sorted Linked Lists
topics: Two PointersConstraints: 0 ≤ number of nodes in each list ≤ 50
description: Given the heads of two sorted linked lists, merge them into one sorted linked list by splicing their nodes together. Return the head of the merged list.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Find the Middle of a Linked List
description: Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node. If there are two middle nodes, return the second one.
examples: Input: [1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5]Output: Node 3Input: [1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6]Output: Node 4Hint: Use the slow/fast pointer technique. When fast reaches the end, slow is at the middle.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Trees & Binary Search
description: Prepare and solve the coding question: Trees & Binary Search asked in JPMORGAN recruitment.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid BST. For every node, all values in its left subtree must be strictly less than its value, and all values in its right subtree must be strictly greater.
examples: Input: [5, 1, 7] → false (right child 7 is correct, but example with [5, 1, 4, null, null, 3, 6] → false because 4 < 5 violates the BST property at root)Hint: Pass min and max bounds through recursion. Avoid the common mistake of only comparing parent-child pairs.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Level Order Traversal of Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return its level-order traversal as a list of lists, where each inner list contains values at that level from left to right.
examples: Input: root = [3, 9, 20, null, null, 15, 7]Output: [[3], [9, 20], [15, 7]]Hint: Use a queue (BFS). At the start of each level, record the current queue size to know how many nodes to process for that level.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
description: Design an algorithm to serialize a binary tree into a string and deserialize the string back into the original tree structure.
examples: Input: root = [1, 2, 3, null, null, 4, 5]Serialized: "1,2,3,#,#,4,5"Deserialized: Original tree reconstructed exactly.Note: Your encoding scheme is your choice — level-order with null markers is the most common approach.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description: Given a sorted array rotated at an unknown pivot index, and a target integer, return the index of target if it exists, otherwise -1. Your solution must run in O(log n) time.
examples: Input: nums = [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2], target = 0Output: 4Hint: At each step of binary search, determine which half is sorted. The target must be in the sorted half if it falls within that range.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
description: Given a sorted array and a target, return [first_index, last_index] of the target. Return [-1, -1] if not found.
examples: Input: nums = [5, 7, 7, 8, 8, 10], target = 8Output: [3, 4]Hint: Run binary search twice — once biased toward the left boundary, once toward the right.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Graphs
description: Prepare and solve the coding question: Graphs asked in JPMORGAN recruitment.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Number of Islands
description: Given a 2D grid of '1' (land) and '0' (water), count the number of islands. An island is a group of adjacent land cells (horizontally or vertically connected) surrounded by water.
examples: Input:11110110101100000000Output: 1Hint: For each unvisited '1', launch a DFS/BFS to mark all connected land as visited. Each launch = one island.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Course Schedule (Cycle Detection)
description: Given numCourses courses and a list of prerequisite pairs [a, b] (take b before a), determine if it is possible to complete all courses. Return true if possible, false if a cycle exists.
examples: Input: numCourses = 2, prerequisites = [[1, 0]]Output: trueInput: numCourses = 2, prerequisites = [[1, 0], [0, 1]]Output: false (circular dependency)
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Word Search in Grid
description: Given a 2D board of characters and a string word, return true if word exists in the grid formed by sequentially adjacent cells (up/down/left/right). The same cell may not be used more than once per path.
examples: Input: board = [["A","B","C","E"],["S","F","C","S"],["A","D","E","E"]], word = "ABCCED"Output: trueHint: Use DFS with backtracking. Mark cells as visited in-place and restore them after recursion.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Coin Change
description: Given an array of coin denominations and an amount, return the minimum number of coins needed to make the amount. Return -1 if it is impossible.
examples: Input: coins = [1, 5, 6, 9], amount = 11Output: 2 (5 + 6 = 11)Input: coins = [2], amount = 3Output: -1
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
topics: strings contain only lowercase English letters.
description: Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence. A subsequence does not need to be contiguous.
examples: Input: text1 = "abcde", text2 = "ace"Output: 3 (the LCS is "ace")Hint: Define dp[i][j] = LCS length of text1[0..i-1] and text2[0..j-1]. Recurrence: if chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1; else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Maximum Subarray (Kadane's Algorithm)
description: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum and return that sum.
examples: Input: nums = [-2, 1, -3, 4, -1, 2, 1, -5, 4]Output: 6 (subarray [4, -1, 2, 1])Hint: Track current_sum and max_sum. If current_sum < 0, reset to 0.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Climbing Stairs
description: You are climbing n stairs. Each time you can climb 1 or 2 steps. Return the number of distinct ways to reach the top.
examples: Input: n = 5Output: 8 (the pattern follows Fibonacci: dp[n] = dp[n-1] + dp[n-2])
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.
examples: Input: nums = [10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18]Output: 4 (the LIS is [2, 3, 7, 101])Hint: The O(n log n) solution uses patience sorting with a tails array and binary search.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Maximum Product Subarray
description: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous subarray with the largest product and return the product.
examples: Input: nums = [2, 3, -2, 4]Output: 6 (subarray [2, 3])Hint: Track both max_product and min_product (because multiplying two negatives yields a positive).
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Heap & Priority Queue
description: Prepare and solve the coding question: Heap & Priority Queue asked in JPMORGAN recruitment.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Top K Frequent Elements
description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequently occurring elements in any order.
examples: Input: nums = [1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3], k = 2Output: [1, 2]Hint: Count frequencies with a hash map, then use a min-heap of size k to maintain the top-k elements.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: K-th Largest Element in an Array
description: Given an integer array nums and integer k, return the k-th largest element (not the k-th distinct).
examples: Input: nums = [3, 2, 1, 5, 6, 4], k = 2Output: 5Hint: A min-heap of size k gives O(n log k). Quickselect gives O(n) average.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Design & Data Structures
description: Prepare and solve the coding question: Design & Data Structures asked in JPMORGAN recruitment.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Implement LRU Cache
topics: Hash Map + Doubly Linked ListConstraints: 1 ≤ capacity ≤ 3000
both get and put must run in O(1) average.
description: Design a data structure implementing a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache. Implement get(key) (return value or -1 if not found) and put(key, value) (insert/update, evicting LRU entry when at capacity).
examples: LRUCache cache = new LRUCache(2);cache.put(1, 1); cache.put(2, 2);cache.get(1); → 1cache.put(3, 3); (evicts key 2)cache.get(2); → -1Hint: Combine a hash map (for O(1) access) with a doubly linked list (for O(1) insertion/deletion at both ends).
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Find Duplicate Number
topics: exactly one duplicate — do not modify array
description: Given an array of n+1 integers where each value is in [1, n], find the one duplicate number without modifying the array.
examples: Input: nums = [1, 3, 4, 2, 2]Output: 2Hint: Treat array indices as a linked list (index → nums[index]). A cycle exists at the duplicate. Apply Floyd's tortoise and hare algorithm.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Subsets (Power Set)
description: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution must not contain duplicate subsets.
examples: Input: nums = [1, 2, 3]Output: [[], [1], [2], [3], [1,2], [1,3], [2,3], [1,2,3]]Hint: Use iterative approach — for each new element, add it to every existing subset.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Finance-Flavored Coding Problems
description: JP Morgan uniquely includes problems inspired by financial systems. These are commonly reported in OA rounds and domain interviews.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock — Multiple Transactions
description: Given a stock price array prices where prices[i] is the price on day i, find the maximum profit you can achieve by completing as many transactions as you like (you must sell before buying again).
examples: Input: prices = [7, 1, 5, 3, 6, 4]Output: 7
approach: Buy at 1, sell at 5 (profit 4). Buy at 3, sell at 6 (profit 3). Total = 7.Insight: Any upward slope contributes to profit — sum all positive day-to-day differences.

title: Maximum Profit with at Most K Transactions
description: Given a stock price array and an integer k representing the maximum number of transactions allowed (buy + sell = 1 transaction), find the maximum profit. You must sell before buying again.
examples: Input: k = 2, prices = [3, 2, 6, 5, 0, 3]Output: 7
approach: Buy at 2, sell at 6 (profit 4). Buy at 0, sell at 3 (profit 3). Total = 7.Hint: Define dp[i][j] = max profit using at most i transactions through day j. Handle the edge case where k ≥ n/2 separately (unlimited transactions).

title: Transaction Fee — Maximize Stock Profit
description: Given an array prices and a transaction fee, find the maximum profit by completing as many transactions as desired (each transaction incurs a fee applied once per buy-sell cycle). You cannot hold more than one share at a time.
examples: Input: prices = [1, 3, 2, 8, 4, 9], fee = 2Output: 8
approach: Transactions: buy at 1 sell at 8 (profit 5), buy at 4 sell at 9 (profit 3). Total = 8.Hint: Maintain two states — cash (max profit holding no stock) and hold (max profit holding one stock). Transition at each price.

title: Running Median of a Data Stream
topics: at most 5×10⁴ calls to addNum and findMedian.
description: Design a data structure that supports adding integers from a data stream and finding the median of all elements added so far, in O(log n) per add and O(1) per query.
examples: addNum(1) → median = 1.0addNum(2) → median = 1.5addNum(3) → median = 2.0
approach: Maintain two heaps — a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half — keeping them balanced in size at all times.Real-world relevance: This pattern appears in real-time financial analytics where running statistics on a price stream are required.

title: Rate Limiter — Sliding Window Counter
description: Design a rate limiter that allows at most N requests per T seconds for a given user. Implement two methods: isAllowed(userId, timestamp) — returns true if the request at the given timestamp should be allowed, false otherwise.
examples: Rate limit: 3 requests per 10 seconds.isAllowed("user1", 1) → true (1 request)isAllowed("user1", 5) → true (2 requests)isAllowed("user1", 9) → true (3 requests)isAllowed("user1", 10) → false (still 3 within window [1, 10])isAllowed("user1", 12) → true (window shifts, request at 1 falls out)Real-world relevance: JP Morgan's trading APIs enforce per-client rate limits to prevent market manipulation and ensure fair access.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Order Book Matching Engine (Simulation)
description: Simulate a simple stock order book. You receive a stream of buy orders (with price and quantity) and sell orders (with price and quantity). Match orders using the rule: a buy order matches a sell order when the buy price ≥ sell price. Process orders in FIFO order for equal prices. For each matched trade, output the trade price and quantity. Return all remaining unmatched orders at the end.
examples: Orders: BUY 100 shares at $50, SELL 100 shares at $48, BUY 50 shares at $45, SELL 50 shares at $52Matched: 100 shares at $48 (buy $50 ≥ sell $48)Remaining: BUY 50 @ $45, SELL 50 @ $52 (no match since $45 < $52)Hint: Use a max-heap for buy orders (highest price has priority) and a min-heap for sell orders (lowest price has priority).Real-world relevance: This is the core of a stock exchange matching engine — a fundamental system at any investment bank's trading technology division.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Detect Arbitrage Opportunity (Currency Exchange)
description: Given an n×n matrix rates where rates[i][j] is the exchange rate from currency i to currency j, determine if an arbitrage opportunity exists (a cycle where you can start with 1 unit of currency X, convert through a sequence of currencies, and end up with more than 1 unit of currency X).
examples: rates =USD → EUR: 0.9, EUR → GBP: 0.8, GBP → USD: 1.5Product: 0.9 × 0.8 × 1.5 = 1.08 > 1 → Arbitrage exists!Hint: Take the negative log of each rate. An arbitrage cycle becomes a negative-weight cycle. Apply Bellman-Ford to detect it.Real-world relevance: Quantitative developers and algo trading teams at JP Morgan regularly build systems to identify and act on arbitrage opportunities across markets.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.

title: Portfolio Rebalancing
description: You are given a portfolio of n assets, each with a current allocation percentage and a target allocation percentage. To rebalance, you can transfer value between assets. Each transfer has a cost equal to 0.1% of the amount transferred. Find the minimum total cost to rebalance the portfolio to the target allocation, given a total portfolio value of V.
examples: V = 100,000Assets: [A: current 40%, target 30%], [B: current 20%, target 35%], [C: current 40%, target 35%]Excess: A = 10,000, C = 5,000. Deficit: B = 15,000.Transfers: A→B 10,000 (cost 10), C→B 5,000 (cost 5). Total cost = $15.Hint: Compute excess and deficit for each asset. Greedily match surpluses to deficits. The minimum total transferred amount (= total surplus = total deficit) determines cost.
approach: Understand the problem statement, identify optimal data structures, and implement with clean code.
